nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, shaded forest streams and small rivers; soft, blackwater or near-blackwater conditions with leaf litter, submerged roots, and sandy or muddy substrate

decorPreferences: caves (coconut shells, clay pots, purpose-made cichlid caves) with small openings sized for the female
sand or fine-gravel substrate for burrowing
driftwood, roots, and bogwood for territory definition
Anubias and other robust plants anchored to hardscape
leaf litter for naturalistic cover and tannin release

diet.notes: Accepts a varied diet of quality dried foods (small cichlid pellets, flake), frozen foods (bloodworm, brine shrimp, daphnia, mysis), and live foods. Occasional vegetable matter (blanched spinach, spirulina-based foods) should be offered. Feed small portions 2–3 times daily; avoid overfeeding as the species is susceptible to internal parasites if kept in poor water quality.

goodWith: peaceful mid- and upper-water tetras (Congo tetra, small characins)
small schooling barbs that stay away from the bottom
Corydoras catfish (robust species, not delicate pygmy cories)
other non-Pelvicachromis bottom dwellers if space permits
avoidWith: any other Pelvicachromis species (hybridization risk; severe aggression during spawning)
Wallaceochromis signatus and other former Pelvicachromis now in Wallaceochromis (same hybridization risk)
Pelvicachromis pulcher, P. taeniatus, P. sacrimontis (must never be mixed with P. rubrolabiatus)
aggressive or large cichlids that will bully the pair
small shrimp and nano fish (fry and egg predation risk)
slow-moving, long-finned fish that cannot escape territorial charges

summary: The Red-Lipped Kribensis is a rare West African dwarf cichlid from Guinea’s Kolentè River basin, distinguished by vivid red lips in males. Slightly larger and more territorial than P. pulcher, it is a devoted cave-spawning monogamous pair that must never be housed with other Pelvicachromis due to hybridization risk.
careNotes:
Pelvicachromis rubrolabiatus (also traded as the Red-Lipped Kribensis and reclassified as Wallaceochromis rubrolabiatus in 2016, though the original name remains in common hobby use) is a relatively rare and demanding West African dwarf cichlid described by Lamboj in 2004. It originates from the Badi River in the Kolentè River basin of Guinea, inhabiting soft, shaded forest streams with acidic conditions. Males are recognised by conspicuous red lips — the species name rubrolabiatus is Latin for “red-lipped” — and by seven dark vertical bars on the body; male fins are notably plain compared to other kribs. Females are somewhat smaller and display the pinkish-silver belly typical of breeding female Pelvicachromis.

A bonded pair can be maintained in a tank of at least 80 L with a 90 cm footprint. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 2–10) is strongly preferred; breeding is unlikely above pH 7.0 and some accounts suggest pH below 6.0 optimises conditioning. Use reverse-osmosis water blended with dechlorinated tap water if local supply is hard. Tannin-releasing driftwood and leaf litter help acidify the tank and provide visual security. Multiple caves are essential — the entrance should be barely large enough for the female to pass through. Sand substrate allows natural burrowing behaviour. Dim or diffused lighting reduces stress and encourages natural foraging.

This species is notably more aggressive and territorial than the common krib (P. pulcher), particularly during spawning. It must not be kept alongside any other Pelvicachromis or Wallaceochromis species: hybridisation is a serious concern across the genus and mixing is considered a significant husbandry error in the cichlid hobby. Compatible dither fish (Congo tetras, small characins, upper-water barbs) help reduce the pair’s territorial fixation on each other. P. rubrolabiatus is rarely available in the trade; it is primarily kept by specialist cichlid enthusiasts.
breedingNotes: P. rubrolabiatus is a monogamous cave-spawning pair-bonder. Condition the pair on varied live and frozen foods before attempting breeding. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.0) and a snug cave with a small entrance trigger spawning; some advanced keepers report success only below pH 6.0. The female deposits eggs inside the cave and performs most of the fanning; the male guards the territory outside. Incubation takes approximately 2–3 days; the female continues to tend larvae until they become free-swimming (roughly 7–10 days post-hatch). Once free-swimming, both parents actively herd and defend the fry. First foods: Artemia nauplii, microworms, and finely crushed dry food. Fry are sensitive to water quality deterioration; small, frequent water changes with appropriately conditioned water are advised during rearing. Remove the pair from the fry tank only if parental aggression becomes a problem, which is uncommon.

primary.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pelvicachromis rubrolabiatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Kolentè River basin, Guinea), water chemistry (soft, pH below 6.0, temperature 24–26°C), minimum tank (90 cm), diet (omnivore), breeding overview (cave-spawner, female guards eggs), and notable characteristic (red lips, larger than congeners, more aggressive than P. pulcher). Confirmed hybridization concern with congeners.

additional.name: FishBase — Wallaceochromis rubrolabiatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (6.5 cm SL male, equating to ~8–10 cm TL), native range (Badi River, Kolente basin, Guinea), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), habitat (freshwater, benthopelagic, tropical), breeding note (pair-bonding, cave-breeding), and trophic level (3.3, omnivore-consistent). Notes reclassification to Wallaceochromis.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Wallaceochromis rubrolabiatus
additional.notes: Provided temperature range (23–26°C), pH range (6.5–7.0; note: conservative relative to Practical Fishkeeping’s “below 6.0”; both ranges cited in careNotes), gH (5–8), adult size (9–10 cm), minimum tank length (100 cm), tank setup details (caves, sand substrate, driftwood, subdued lighting), and breeding notes (fry fed Artemia nauplii and crushed dry food).

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Keeping Kribs: The Culture of Pelvicachromis Cichlids
additional.notes: Provided species context within the genus, confirmed red lips as the key distinguishing character (rubrolabiatus = Latin for red-lipped), noted seven dark vertical bars on body, plain male fins, native habitat (soft acidic forest streams of Kolentè basin), omnivore diet, and description of P. rubrolabiatus as notably more territorial and hostile than P. pulcher.

additional.name: Capital Cichlid Association Forum — Wallaceochromis rubrolabiatus keeper experience
additional.notes: Hobbyist keeper accounts confirming extreme intra-pair aggression before pairing is established, recommendation for abundant hiding spots, note that pH below 5.0 has been used by some breeders for spawning induction, and fungal/bacterial infection susceptibility. Supplementary source; used to validate careNotes on water quality sensitivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawning monogamous pair. Female guards eggs and larvae; male defends territory. Both parents care for free-swimming fry. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.0) required for reliable spawning. Fry raised on Artemia nauplii and fine crushed dry food.
